sentence: Subjective:
The patient, Mrs. King, is a 36-year-old female, date of birth 1/5/1986, with a medical record number of Q227631.
She presented for evaluation of acid reflux.
She reports a history of anxiety and seasonal allergies.
She states that her primary doctor, Dr. Scott, referred her to my office.
She has been experiencing acid reflux since she was around 12 years old, but it has worsened over the past few years.
She is currently taking 40 mg of omeprazole twice daily.
She reports that some days she feels fine, but others are severely affected.
She describes the sensation as burning in her chest and throat.
She denies difficulty swallowing, abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, changes in bowel movements, constipation, diarrhea, and rectal bleeding.
She attributes her symptoms to stress and food choices, particularly spicy foods, carbonated beverages, citrus, and tomato-based foods.
Past medical history:
The patient has a history of anxiety and seasonal allergies.
Review of systems:
The patient denies symptoms in other organs that are relevant to her initial presentation.
Current medications:
She is currently taking omeprazole 40 mg twice daily.
Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]
Physical exam: A well-healed abdominal surgical scar is noted.
The patient has a normal physical exam per my new patient template.
Test Results: [NOT MENTIONED]
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: I suspect that the patient likely has gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) with underlying functional etiology.
Problem list: GERD; suspected functional etiology.
Plan:
I recommended that the patient change her diet to avoid trigger foods like spicy foods, carbonated beverages, citrus, and tomato-based foods.
I also advised her to work on stress management to address her anxiety.
I prescribed Nexium 40 mg twice daily to replace her current omeprazole.
I also recommended an upper endoscopy (EGD) procedure to look for potential causes of her GERD.

The procedure will involve sedation and a small camera passed through her mouth down her throat, through her stomach, and into the small intestines to look for ulcers or signs of precancerous or cancerous conditions.
Biopsies will be taken if anything suspicious is found.
I instructed her to schedule the procedure at the checkout desk and to follow up with me in the office to discuss the results and the effectiveness of the dietary and stress management changes.
I also instructed her to call my office if anything changes or if she has problems before the EGd and follow-up appointment.
Follow-up:
I instructed the patient to follow up with me in the office to discuss the results of the EGD and to discuss the effectiveness of the dietary and stress management changes.
